From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Medical symptom or condition

Not to be confused with [malaise](/wiki/Malaise "Malaise"). For other uses, see [Nausea (disambiguation)](/wiki/Nausea_(disambiguation) "Nausea (disambiguation)").

Medical condition

| Nausea | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| A 1681 painting depicting a person [vomiting](/wiki/Vomiting "Vomiting") | |
| [Specialty](/wiki/Medical_specialty "Medical specialty") | [Gastroenterology](/wiki/Gastroenterology "Gastroenterology") |
| [Complications](/wiki/Complication_(medicine) "Complication (medicine)") | [Vomiting](/wiki/Vomiting "Vomiting") |

**Nausea** is a diffuse sensation of unease and discomfort, sometimes perceived as an urge to [vomit](/wiki/Vomiting "Vomiting").[[1]](#cite_note-AFP-1) It can be a debilitating symptom if prolonged and has been described as placing discomfort on the chest, abdomen, or back of the throat.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Over 30 definitions of nausea were proposed in a 2011 book on the topic.[[3]](#cite_note-newrev2017-3)

Nausea is a [non-specific symptom](/wiki/Non-specific_symptom "Non-specific symptom"), which means that it has many possible causes. Some common causes of nausea are [gastroenteritis](/wiki/Gastroenteritis "Gastroenteritis") and other [gastrointestinal disorders](/wiki/Gastrointestinal_disease "Gastrointestinal disease"), [food poisoning](/wiki/Food_poisoning "Food poisoning"), [motion sickness](/wiki/Motion_sickness "Motion sickness"), [dizziness](/wiki/Dizziness "Dizziness"), [migraine](/wiki/Migraine "Migraine"), [fainting](/wiki/Fainting "Fainting"), [low blood sugar](/wiki/Low_blood_sugar "Low blood sugar"), [anxiety](/wiki/Anxiety "Anxiety"), [hyperthermia](/wiki/Hyperthermia "Hyperthermia"), [dehydration](/wiki/Dehydration "Dehydration") and [lack of sleep](/wiki/Sleep_deprivation "Sleep deprivation"). Nausea is a side effect of many medications including [chemotherapy](/wiki/Chemotherapy "Chemotherapy"), or [morning sickness](/wiki/Morning_sickness "Morning sickness") in early pregnancy. Nausea may also be caused by [disgust](/wiki/Disgust "Disgust") and [depression](/wiki/Depression_(mood) "Depression (mood)").[[1]](#cite_note-AFP-1)

Medications taken to prevent and treat nausea and vomiting are called [antiemetics](/wiki/Antiemetic "Antiemetic"). The most commonly prescribed antiemetics in the US are [promethazine](/wiki/Promethazine "Promethazine"), [metoclopramide](/wiki/Metoclopramide "Metoclopramide"), and the newer [ondansetron](/wiki/Ondansetron "Ondansetron"). The word nausea is from [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *nausea*, from [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") ναυσία – *nausia*,[[4]](#cite_note-4) "ναυτία" – *nautia*, [seasickness](/wiki/Motion_sickness "Motion sickness"), "feeling sick or queasy",[[5]](#cite_note-5) from Latin *naus*, Greek ναος, a ship.

## Causes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nausea&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Causes")]

[Gastrointestinal infections](/wiki/Gastroenteritis "Gastroenteritis") (37%) and [food poisoning](/wiki/Food_poisoning "Food poisoning") are the two most common causes of acute nausea and vomiting.[[1]](#cite_note-AFP-1) Side effects from medications (3%) and pregnancy are also relatively frequent.[[1]](#cite_note-AFP-1) There are many causes of chronic nausea.[[1]](#cite_note-AFP-1) Nausea and vomiting remain undiagnosed in 10% of the cases. Aside from morning sickness, there are no sex differences in complaints of nausea. After childhood, doctor consultations decrease steadily with age. Only a fraction of one percent of doctor visits by those over 65 are due to nausea.[[6]](#cite_note-AFPHelena07-6)

### Gastrointestinal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nausea&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Gastrointestinal")]

Gastrointestinal infection is one of the most common causes of acute nausea and vomiting.[[1]](#cite_note-AFP-1) Chronic nausea may be the presentation of many gastrointestinal disorders, occasionally as the major symptom, such as [gastroesophageal reflux disease](/wiki/Gastroesophageal_reflux_disease "Gastroesophageal reflux disease"), [functional dyspepsia](/wiki/Functional_dyspepsia "Functional dyspepsia"), [gastritis](/wiki/Gastritis "Gastritis"), [biliary reflux](/wiki/Biliary_reflux "Biliary reflux"), [gastroparesis](/wiki/Gastroparesis "Gastroparesis"), [peptic ulcer](/wiki/Peptic_ulcer "Peptic ulcer"), [celiac disease](/wiki/Celiac_disease "Celiac disease"), [non-celiac gluten sensitivity](/wiki/Non-celiac_gluten_sensitivity "Non-celiac gluten sensitivity"), [Crohn's disease](/wiki/Crohn%27s_disease "Crohn's disease"), [hepatitis](/wiki/Hepatitis "Hepatitis"), [upper gastrointestinal](/wiki/Gastrointestinal_tract#Upper_gastrointestinal_tract "Gastrointestinal tract") malignancy, and [pancreatic cancer](/wiki/Pancreatic_cancer "Pancreatic cancer").[[1]](#cite_note-AFP-1)[[7]](#cite_note-VoltaCaio2017-7) Uncomplicated *[Helicobacter pylori](/wiki/Helicobacter_pylori "Helicobacter pylori")* infection does not cause chronic nausea.[[1]](#cite_note-AFP-1)

### Food poisoning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nausea&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Food poisoning")]

[Food poisoning](/wiki/Food_poisoning "Food poisoning") usually causes an abrupt onset of nausea and vomiting one to six hours after ingestion of contaminated food and lasts for one to two days.[[8]](#cite_note-USA07-8) It is due to toxins produced by [bacteria](/wiki/Bacteria "Bacteria") in food.[[8]](#cite_note-USA07-8)

### Medications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nausea&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Medications")]

Many medications can potentially cause nausea.[[8]](#cite_note-USA07-8) Some of the most frequently associated include cytotoxic [chemotherapy](/wiki/Chemotherapy "Chemotherapy") regimens for cancer and other diseases, and [general anaesthetic agents](/wiki/General_anaesthesia "General anaesthesia"). An old cure for migraine, [ergotamine](/wiki/Ergotamine "Ergotamine"), is well known to cause devastating nausea in some patients; a person using it for the first time will be prescribed an [antiemetic](/wiki/Antiemetic "Antiemetic") for relief if needed.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Opioids](/wiki/Opioids "Opioids") commonly cause nausea too.

### Pregnancy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nausea&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Pregnancy")]

Nausea or "[morning sickness](/wiki/Morning_sickness "Morning sickness")" is common during early [pregnancy](/wiki/Pregnancy "Pregnancy") but may occasionally continue into the second and third trimesters. In the first trimester nearly 80 % of women have some degree of nausea.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Pregnancy should therefore be considered as a possible cause of nausea in any sexually active woman of child-bearing age.[[8]](#cite_note-USA07-8) While usually it is mild and self-limiting, severe cases known as [hyperemesis gravidarum](/wiki/Hyperemesis_gravidarum "Hyperemesis gravidarum") may require treatment.[[10]](#cite_note-preg07-10)

### Disequilibrium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nausea&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Disequilibrium")]

A number of conditions involving balance such as [motion sickness](/wiki/Motion_sickness "Motion sickness") and [vertigo](/wiki/Vertigo_(medical) "Vertigo (medical)") can lead to nausea and vomiting.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Gynecologic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nausea&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Gynecologic")]

[Dysmenorrhea](/wiki/Dysmenorrhea "Dysmenorrhea") can cause nausea.[[12]](#cite_note-AFP2014-12)

### Psychiatric

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nausea&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Psychiatric")]

Nausea may be caused by [depression](/wiki/Depression_(mood) "Depression (mood)"), [anxiety disorders](/wiki/Anxiety_disorder "Anxiety disorder") and [eating disorders](/wiki/Eating_disorder "Eating disorder").[[13]](#cite_note-SinghYoon2016table1-13)

### Potentially serious

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nausea&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Potentially serious")]

While most causes of nausea are not serious, some serious conditions are associated with nausea. These include [pancreatitis](/wiki/Pancreatitis "Pancreatitis"), [small bowel obstruction](/wiki/Small_bowel_obstruction "Small bowel obstruction"), [appendicitis](/wiki/Appendicitis "Appendicitis"), [cholecystitis](/wiki/Cholecystitis "Cholecystitis"), [hepatitis](/wiki/Hepatitis "Hepatitis"), [Addisonian crisis](/wiki/Addisonian_crisis "Addisonian crisis"), [diabetic ketoacidosis](/wiki/Diabetic_ketoacidosis "Diabetic ketoacidosis"), increased [intracranial pressure](/wiki/Intracranial_pressure "Intracranial pressure"), [spontaneous intracranial hypotension](/wiki/Spontaneous_intracranial_hypotension "Spontaneous intracranial hypotension"), [brain tumors](/wiki/Brain_tumor "Brain tumor"), [meningitis](/wiki/Meningitis "Meningitis"), [heart attack](/wiki/Heart_attack "Heart attack"), [rabies](/wiki/Rabies "Rabies"),[[14]](#cite_note-14) [carbon monoxide poisoning](/wiki/Carbon_monoxide_poisoning "Carbon monoxide poisoning") and many others.[[1]](#cite_note-AFP-1)

## Pathophysiology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nausea&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Pathophysiology")]

Research on nausea and vomiting has relied on using animal models to mimic the anatomy and neuropharmacologic features of the human body.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The physiologic mechanism of nausea is a complex process that has yet to be fully elucidated. There are four general pathways that are activated by specific triggers in the human body that go on to create the sensation of nausea and vomiting.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

* [Central nervous system](/wiki/Central_nervous_system "Central nervous system") (CNS): Stimuli can affect areas of the CNS including the cerebral cortex and the [limbic system](/wiki/Limbic_system "Limbic system"). These areas are activated by elevated intracranial pressure, irritation of the meninges (i.e. blood or infection), and extreme emotional triggers such as anxiety. The [supratentorial region](/wiki/Supratentorial_region "Supratentorial region") is also responsible for the sensation of nausea.[[3]](#cite_note-newrev2017-3)
* [Chemoreceptor trigger zone](/wiki/Chemoreceptor_trigger_zone "Chemoreceptor trigger zone") (CTZ): The CTZ is located in the [area postrema](/wiki/Area_postrema "Area postrema") in the floor of the fourth ventricle within the brain. This area is outside the blood brain barrier, and is therefore readily exposed to substances circulating through the blood and cerebral spinal fluid. Common triggers of the CTZ include metabolic abnormalities, toxins, and medications. Activation of the CTZ is mediated by [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine") (D2) receptors, [serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin") (5HT3) receptors, and [neurokinin](/wiki/Neurokinin "Neurokinin") receptors (NK1).
* [Vestibular system](/wiki/Vestibular_system "Vestibular system"): This system is activated by disturbances to the vestibular apparatus in the inner ear. These include movements that cause motion sickness and dizziness. This pathway is triggered via [histamine](/wiki/Histamine "Histamine") (H1) receptors and [acetylcholine](/wiki/Acetylcholine "Acetylcholine") (ACh) receptors.
* Peripheral Pathways: These pathways are triggered via chemoreceptors and mechanoreceptors in the gastrointestinal tract, as well as other organs such as the heart and kidneys. Common activators of these pathways include toxins present in the gastrointestinal lumen and distension of the gastrointestinal lumen from blockage or dysmotility of the bowels. Signals from these pathways travel via multiple neural tracts including the [vagus](/wiki/Vagus_nerve "Vagus nerve"), [glossopharyngeal](/wiki/Glossopharyngeal_nerve "Glossopharyngeal nerve"), [splanchnic](/wiki/Splanchnic "Splanchnic"), and [sympathetic](/wiki/Sympathetic_nervous_system "Sympathetic nervous system") nerves.

Signals from any of these pathways then travel to the brainstem, activating several structures including the [nucleus of the solitary tract](/wiki/Solitary_nucleus "Solitary nucleus"), the [dorsal motor nucleus](/wiki/Dorsal_nucleus_of_vagus_nerve "Dorsal nucleus of vagus nerve") of the vagus, and [central pattern generator](/wiki/Central_pattern_generator "Central pattern generator").[[21]](#cite_note-21) These structures go on to signal various downstream effects of nausea and vomiting. The body's motor muscle responses involve halting the muscles of the gastrointestinal tract, and in fact causing reversed propulsion of gastric contents towards the mouth while increasing abdominal muscle contraction. Autonomic effects involve increased salivation and the sensation of feeling faint that often occurs with nausea and vomiting.

### Pre-nausea pathophysiology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nausea&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Pre-nausea pathophysiology")]

It has been described that alterations in heart rate can occur as well as the release of [vasopressin](/wiki/Vasopressin "Vasopressin") from the [posterior pituitary](/wiki/Posterior_pituitary "Posterior pituitary").[[3]](#cite_note-newrev2017-3)

## Diagnosis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nausea&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Diagnosis")]

### Patient history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nausea&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Patient history")]

Taking a thorough patient history may reveal important clues to the cause of nausea and vomiting. If the patient's symptoms have an acute onset, then drugs, toxins, and infections are likely. In contrast, a long-standing history of nausea will point towards a chronic illness as the culprit. The timing of nausea and vomiting after eating food is an important factor to pay attention to. Symptoms that occur within an hour of eating may indicate an obstruction proximal to the small intestine, such as [gastroparesis](/wiki/Gastroparesis "Gastroparesis") or [pyloric stenosis](/wiki/Pyloric_stenosis "Pyloric stenosis"). An obstruction further down in the intestine or colon will cause delayed vomiting. An infectious cause of nausea and vomiting such as gastroenteritis may present several hours to days after the food was ingested.[[15]](#cite_note-H2015-15) The contents of the emesis is a valuable clue towards determining the cause. Bits of fecal matter in the emesis indicate obstruction in the distal intestine or the colon. Emesis that is of a bilious nature (greenish in color) localizes the obstruction to a point past the stomach. Emesis of undigested food points to an obstruction prior to the gastric outlet, such as [achalasia](/wiki/Achalasia "Achalasia") or [Zenker's diverticulum](/wiki/Zenker%27s_diverticulum "Zenker's diverticulum"). If patient experiences reduced abdominal pain after vomiting, then obstruction is a likely etiology. However, vomiting does not relieve the pain brought on by [pancreatitis](/wiki/Pancreatitis "Pancreatitis") or [cholecystitis](/wiki/Cholecystitis "Cholecystitis").[[15]](#cite_note-H2015-15)

### Physical exam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nausea&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Physical exam")]

It is important to watch out for signs of dehydration, such as [orthostatic hypotension](/wiki/Orthostatic_hypotension "Orthostatic hypotension") and loss of skin [turgor](/wiki/Turgor "Turgor"). Auscultation of the abdomen can produce several clues to the cause of nausea and vomiting. A high-pitched tinkling sound indicates possible [bowel obstruction](/wiki/Bowel_obstruction "Bowel obstruction"), while a splashing "[succussion](/wiki/Succussion_splash "Succussion splash")" sound is more indicative of gastric outlet obstruction. Eliciting pain on the abdominal exam when pressing on the patient may indicate an inflammatory process. Signs such as papilledema, visual field losses, or focal neurological deficits are red flag signs for elevated intracranial pressure.[[15]](#cite_note-H2015-15)

### Diagnostic testing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nausea&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Diagnostic testing")]

When a history and physical exam are not enough to determine the cause of nausea and vomiting, certain diagnostic tests may prove useful. A chemistry panel would be useful for electrolyte and metabolic abnormalities.[[22]](#cite_note-:1-22) Liver function tests and lipase would identify pancreaticobiliary diseases.[[22]](#cite_note-:1-22) Abdominal X-rays showing air-fluid levels indicate bowel obstruction, while an X-ray showing air-filled bowel loops are more indicative of [ileus](/wiki/Ileus "Ileus"). More advanced imaging and procedures may be necessary, such as a CT scan, upper endoscopy, colonoscopy, barium enema, or MRI. Abnormal GI motility can be assessed using specific tests like gastric scintigraphy, wireless motility capsules, and small-intestinal manometry.[[15]](#cite_note-H2015-15)

## Treatment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nausea&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Treatment")]

If dehydration is present due to loss of fluids from severe vomiting, [rehydration](/wiki/Fluid_replacement "Fluid replacement") with oral electrolyte solutions is preferred.[[1]](#cite_note-AFP-1) If this is not effective or possible, intravenous rehydration may be required.[[1]](#cite_note-AFP-1) Medical care is recommended if: a person cannot keep any liquids down, has symptoms more than 2 days, is weak, has a fever, has stomach pain, vomits more than two times in a day or does not urinate for more than 8 hours.[[23]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-23)

### Medications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nausea&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Medications")]

Numerous pharmacologic medications are available for the treatment of nausea. There is no medication that is clearly superior to other medications for all cases of nausea.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The choice of [antiemetic](/wiki/Antiemetic "Antiemetic") medication may be based on the situation during which the person experiences nausea. For people with motion sickness and vertigo, antihistamines and anticholinergics such as [meclizine](/wiki/Meclizine "Meclizine") and [scopolamine](/wiki/Hyoscine_hydrobromide "Hyoscine hydrobromide") are particularly effective.[[25]](#cite_note-:0-25) Nausea and vomiting associated with migraine headaches respond best to dopamine antagonists such as [metoclopramide](/wiki/Metoclopramide "Metoclopramide"), [prochlorperazine](/wiki/Prochlorperazine "Prochlorperazine"), and [chlorpromazine](/wiki/Chlorpromazine "Chlorpromazine").[[25]](#cite_note-:0-25) In cases of gastroenteritis, serotonin antagonists such as [ondansetron](/wiki/Ondansetron "Ondansetron") were found to suppress nausea and vomiting, as well as reduce the need for IV fluid resuscitation.[[25]](#cite_note-:0-25) The combination of [pyridoxine](/wiki/Pyridoxine "Pyridoxine") and [doxylamine](/wiki/Doxylamine "Doxylamine") is the first line treatment for pregnancy-related nausea and vomiting.[[25]](#cite_note-:0-25) [Dimenhydrinate](/wiki/Dimenhydrinate "Dimenhydrinate") is an inexpensive and effective over the counter medication for preventing postoperative nausea and vomiting.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Other factors to consider when choosing an antiemetic medication include the person's preference, side-effect profile, and cost.

[Nabilone](/wiki/Nabilone "Nabilone") is also indicated for this purpose.

### Alternative medicine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nausea&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Alternative medicine")]

In certain people, [cannabinoids](/wiki/Cannabinoids "Cannabinoids") may be effective in reducing chemotherapy associated nausea and vomiting.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) Several studies have demonstrated the therapeutic effects of cannabinoids for nausea and vomiting in the advanced stages of illnesses such as [cancer](/wiki/Cancer "Cancer") and [AIDS](/wiki/HIV/AIDS "HIV/AIDS").[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)

In hospital settings topical anti-nausea gels are not indicated because of lack of research backing their efficacy.[[31]](#cite_note-AAHPMfive-31) [Topical gels](/wiki/Topical_gels "Topical gels") containing [lorazepam](/wiki/Lorazepam "Lorazepam"), [diphenhydramine](/wiki/Diphenhydramine "Diphenhydramine"), and [haloperidol](/wiki/Haloperidol "Haloperidol") are sometimes used for nausea but are not equivalent to more established therapies.[[31]](#cite_note-AAHPMfive-31)

[Ginger](/wiki/Ginger "Ginger") has also been shown to be potentially effective in treating several types of nausea.[[32]](#cite_note-medind-32)[[33]](#cite_note-ernst-meta-33)

## Prognosis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nausea&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Prognosis")]

The outlook depends on the cause. Most people recover within few hours or a day. While short-term nausea and vomiting are generally harmless, they may sometimes indicate a more serious condition. When associated with prolonged vomiting, it may lead to [dehydration](/wiki/Dehydration "Dehydration") or dangerous [electrolyte](/wiki/Electrolyte "Electrolyte") imbalances or both. Repeated intentional vomiting, characteristic of [bulimia](/wiki/Bulimia "Bulimia"), can cause [stomach acid](/wiki/Stomach_acid "Stomach acid") to wear away at the [enamel](/wiki/Tooth_enamel "Tooth enamel") present on the teeth.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

## Epidemiology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nausea&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Epidemiology")]

Nausea and or [vomiting](/wiki/Vomiting "Vomiting") is the main complaint in 1.6% of visits to family physicians in Australia.[[6]](#cite_note-AFPHelena07-6) However, only 25% of people with nausea visit their family physician.[[1]](#cite_note-AFP-1) In Australia, nausea, as opposed to vomiting, occurs most frequently in persons aged 15–24 years, and is less common in other age groups.[[6]](#cite_note-AFPHelena07-6)
